subject: science
title: science_13.json
description: Grade 4 Science Assessment - Day 13: Magnets and Magnetic Interactions

questions.q: What happens when you place the south pole of one magnet near the north pole of another magnet?
options: They pull toward each other and stick.
They push away from each other.
They lose all of their magnetic power.
They create an electric spark between them.
questions.answer: They pull toward each other and stick.
questions.hint: Remember the basic rule: opposite poles perform this action.
questions.explanation: Opposite magnetic poles (North and South) attract each other, pulling the magnets together.

questions.q: Which of the following items can be picked up by a standard school magnet?
options: An iron nail
A plastic button
A wooden block
A rubber eraser
questions.hint: Magnets only attract certain types of metals.
questions.explanation: Iron is a ferromagnetic material, meaning it is strongly attracted to magnets.

questions.q: What do we call the two ends of a magnet where the magnetic force is the strongest?
questions.hint: Every magnet has a North one and a South one.
questions.explanation: The magnetic forces are concentrated at the ends of a magnet, which are called the magnetic poles.

questions.q: Why does a magnetic compass needle always point toward the geographic North Pole of the Earth?
options: The Earth acts like a giant magnet with its own magnetic field.
The North Pole is the warmest place on Earth, attracting metal.
The sun pulls all magnetic objects toward the top of the globe.
Ocean currents drag the lightweight needle toward the Arctic.
questions.answer: The Earth acts like a giant magnet with its own magnetic field.
questions.hint: Think about what is hidden deep inside the Earth's iron core.
questions.explanation: Earth has a liquid iron outer core that generates a global magnetic field, turning the planet into a giant magnet that interacts with compass needles.

questions.q: If you break a bar magnet perfectly in half, what will you have?
options: Two smaller magnets, each with a north and a south pole
One magnet that is only a north pole and one that is only a south pole
Two pieces of regular metal that are no longer magnetic at all
One working magnet and one piece of non-magnetic scrap metal
questions.answer: Two smaller magnets, each with a north and a south pole
questions.hint: Magnetic poles always exist in matching pairs.
questions.explanation: Breaking a magnet does not separate the poles; instead, new poles form at the broken ends, creating two complete, smaller magnets.

questions.q: What term describes the force that pushes two matching magnetic poles away from each other?
questions.hint: It is the opposite word of attraction.
questions.explanation: Repulsion is the magnetic force that drives like poles (North-North or South-South) apart.

questions.q: Which metal is NOT attracted to a magnet?
options: Aluminum
Iron
Nickel
Cobalt
questions.hint: Think about soda cans or kitchen foil; they do not stick to magnets.
questions.explanation: While iron, nickel, and cobalt are ferromagnetic metals, aluminum is non-magnetic and does not react to a magnet's field.

questions.q: How can you show the invisible magnetic field lines surrounding a bar magnet during a classroom experiment?
options: By sprinkling fine iron filings on a sheet of paper over the magnet
By pouring warm water over the magnet to see the steam lines
By shining a bright purple flashlight directly at the magnet's base
By rubbing the magnet with a piece of silk fabric until it glows
questions.answer: By sprinkling fine iron filings on a sheet of paper over the magnet
questions.hint: Tiny bits of magnetic metal will line up along the force paths.
questions.explanation: Iron filings act like tiny compass needles, aligning themselves along the magnetic field lines to map out the shape of the field.

questions.q: What is a major advantage of using an electromagnet instead of a permanent bar magnet in a junkyard crane?
options: An electromagnet can be turned on and off by controlling the electricity.
An electromagnet never needs a metal core to function.
An electromagnet is completely unaffected by water or rain.
An electromagnet stays magnetic forever without any power source.
questions.answer: An electromagnet can be turned on and off by controlling the electricity.
questions.hint: Think about how a crane needs to drop a heavy car after picking it up.
questions.explanation: Because its magnetic field is produced by an electric current, turning the current off demagnetizes the crane, allowing it to drop picked-up scrap metal easily.

questions.q: What happens to the strength of a magnet's pull as you move an object further away from it?
questions.hint: Think about whether the pull gets weaker or stronger when the distance increases.
questions.explanation: The magnetic force is strongest close to the magnet and drops off significantly as the distance increases.
